pattern: Once the array is sorted, every occurrence of the target value lands in one contiguous block, so counting how many elements are smaller than it tells you exactly where that block starts.
hints: You don't need to actually inspect the sorted array position by position -- think about how many elements end up before the target's block once everything is in order.
Count how many elements of the original array are strictly less than the target; that count is the index of the first occurrence of the target after sorting.
Count how many elements equal the target too -- the answer is that many consecutive indices starting right after the 'strictly less' count, in increasing order.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, target is the largest value
single-element array not equal to target
target not present anywhere, empty result
target is both the minimum and maximum boundary value

stress.description: random arrays with a target drawn from the same value range, so it sometimes appears zero, one, or many times
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 100) for _ in range(n)]
    target = rng.randint(1, 100)
    return [nums, target]
